perturbation.original_text: b) The receiving party shall use the same degree of care as it uses with its own propriety information to protect and maintain the confidentiality of the information of the furnishing party. The receiving party understands that he/she may review sensitive member information and trade secrets, and agrees to maintain confidentiality of all information.
perturbation.changed_text: b) The receiving party shall use a reasonable degree of care to maintain the confidentiality of the information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'same degree of care as it uses with its own propriety information' to 'reasonable degree of care,' it introduces ambiguity. What constitutes 'reasonable' is subjective and opens the door to interpretation. This weakens the protection originally intended, making it difficult to enforce a specific standard of confidentiality. This in-text contradiction can be noticed in section 1(a), where the receiving party should not use the information other than in accordance with the agreement, however a reasonable degree might be interpreted as the receiving party being able to use the information for other purposes without any clear explanation.
perturbation.contradicted_text: a) The information shall be deemed the exclusive property of the furnishing party. The information and all copies thereof shall be returned to the furnishing party upon the furnishing party’s request. The receiving party will not use the information for any purpose other than in accordance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the receiving party must use the same degree of care as it uses with its own proprietary information, setting a clear and objective standard. The changed text replaces this with 'reasonable degree of care,' introducing ambiguity and subjectivity. This weakens the confidentiality protection. Section 1(a) of the contract states that the receiving party will not use the information for any purpose other than in accordance with the terms of the agreement. The 'reasonable degree of care' standard creates an in-text contradiction with section 1(a), because 'reasonable' may be interpreted in a way that permits the receiving party to use the information for other purposes beyond the terms of the agreement, without violating the confidentiality provision. The change makes the agreement unenforceable, and thus meets the requirement for a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: c) The receiving party agrees it will not allow any of its employees, agents, representatives, affiliates or any other person(s) to disclose or use any of the information of the furnishing party, except in accordance with the terms of the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: c) The receiving party agrees to make reasonable efforts to prevent its employees from disclosing information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will not allow' with 'make reasonable efforts' significantly weakens the prohibition on disclosure. The original clause imposed a strict obligation, while the modified clause only requires a level of effort, creating a loophole. It contradicts Section 1(e) where the information can not be disseminated by the receiving party, however this modification allows it to disseminate the information with reasonable effort to prevent it, creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: e) The information is being furnished and/or disclosed for the sole purpose of examining the feasibility of a contract or a business relationship between the individual(s) identified above and the information is not to be disseminated by the receiving party to any other individual or entity and is not to be used by the receiving party for any other purpose
perturbation.justification: The original text stated the receiving party 'will not allow' disclosure, imposing a strict prohibition. The changed text weakens this to 'make reasonable efforts,' creating a loophole. This directly contradicts Section 1(e), which states the information 'is not to be disseminated,' as the modified clause now permits dissemination with 'reasonable effort' to prevent it. This creates a clear and direct conflict, making enforcement unclear and introducing a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: f) All information shall be destroyed or returned to the furnishing party whenever any of the Parties so request in writing or upon termination of this Agreement for any reason. Any Party who disclosed information to another party or entity (e.g., employees, representatives, affiliates or independent contractors) must assure that all such parties or entities return or destroy said information. If any of the Parties so requests in written or upon the termination of this Agreement for any reason.
perturbation.changed_text: f) Upon termination of this Agreement, the receiving party will make good faith efforts to return or destroy the information.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates the destruction or return of all information, whereas this version introduces a 'good faith efforts' standard. This reduces the obligation to a weaker, less enforceable standard. It creates an in-text contradiction because in another part of the agreement it specifies the consequences if the receiving party doesn't comply with this section
perturbation.contradicted_text: d) If the receiving party or any of its employees, agents, representatives, affiliates or any other person(s) shall attempt to use or dispose of any of the information of the furnishing party in a manner contrary to the terms of this Agreement, the furnishing party shall have the right, in addition to such other remedies, which may be available to it, to injunctive relief enjoining such acts or attempts, it being acknowledged that legal remedies are inadequate.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the destruction or return of information, creating a firm obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'good faith efforts,' which introduces ambiguity and makes enforcement less certain. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text' that provides injunctive relief for any use or disposal of information contrary to the agreement's terms. The original text would result in this injunctive relief, while the changed text has a weaker obligation that would make injunctive relief less likely, thus creating a strong, in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: g) The Parties are aware of and understand Member Confidentiality as referred to in Section 264 of the Health Insurance Profitability and Accountability Act of 1996 (HIPPA). All member information is considered highly sensitive and may not be disseminated.
perturbation.changed_text: g) The Parties acknowledge that some member information may be subject to confidentiality requirements.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the explicit reference to HIPAA and the statement that all member information is highly sensitive and may not be disseminated. By saying 'some information may be subject to confidentiality' it implies some might not be, which directly contradicts the original intent and weakens protection. This contradicts the statement in section 1(b) where the receiving party agrees to maintain confidentiality of all information
perturbation.contradicted_text: b) The receiving party shall use the same degree of care as it uses with its own propriety information to protect and maintain the confidentiality of the information of the furnishing party. The receiving party understands that he/she may review sensitive member information and trade secrets, and agrees to maintain confidentiality of all information.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that 'All member information is considered highly sensitive and may not be disseminated,' referring to HIPAA. The changed text weakens this by stating that 'some member information may be subject to confidentiality requirements,' implying that some information may not be confidential. This directly contradicts section 1(b), which states that the receiving party 'agrees to maintain confidentiality of all information.' The modified text introduces an inconsistency regarding whether all or only some member information is confidential, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: h) ​ ​ ACBN Executive Board members, employees, subject-matter experts (SMEs), and all those with access to confidential exam information are prohibited from developing or delivering examination preparation products and are not eligible to take the examination during their tenure and for a period of two years following the end of their access to examination content.
perturbation.changed_text: h) ACBN personnel with access to confidential exam information are discouraged from developing examination preparation products.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'prohibited' to 'discouraged' weakens the restriction on developing exam preparation products. This makes it a recommendation rather than a strict rule. This creates an in-text contradiction since some member information may be subject to confidentiality requirements, this statement allows them to create examination preparation products which might include confidential exam information
perturbation.contradicted_text: g) The Parties acknowledge that some member information may be subject to confidentiality requirements.
perturbation.justification: The original text strictly 'prohibited' certain personnel from developing exam prep products. The changed text weakens this to 'discouraged', effectively allowing those with access to confidential information to develop such products, which potentially contradicts the contradicted text (g) that acknowledges some member information may be subject to confidentiality requirements. The change from 'prohibited' to 'discouraged' directly conflicts with the need to maintain confidentiality, creating a strong in-text contradiction according to the Omissions-In Text Contradiction perturbation definition, as it alters an obligation that conflicts with a constraint in another section.
